home.heroSubtitle: The first all-in-one pregnancy and postpartum platform built for Mauritius. In English, French, and Kreol Morisien.
home.featuresTitle: Everything you need, in one place
home.featuresSubtitle: No more WhatsApp groups, Facebook guessing, or generic international apps. Real support, locally made.
home.featureTrackerDesc: Week-by-week tracking with Mauritius-specific content, local fruit comparisons, and checkup reminders.
home.featureDirectoryDesc: Find gynaecologists, clinics, and maternity wards near you across all districts.
home.featureEmergencyDesc: Instantly find the nearest maternity ward with one tap. When every second counts.
home.featurePPDDesc: PPD mood check-ins, recovery timeline, breastfeeding support, and baby milestone tracker.
home.featureForumDesc: A moderated, safe space for pregnancy questions. Including a solo mothers solidarity network.
home.featureDonationsDesc: Give and receive baby items. Connecting generous Mauritians with mothers in need.
home.featureFoodDesc: Which Mauritian foods are safe during pregnancy? Dal puri, gato pima, dholl — we cover it all.
home.featureRightsDesc: Know your rights under the Workers' Rights Act. 14 weeks paid leave, explained simply.
home.ctaSubtitle: Join thousands of Mauritian mums on their pregnancy journey.

rights.title: Your Maternity Rights
rights.subtitle: Know your rights under Mauritian law
rights.leaveDesc: 14 weeks of paid maternity leave under the Workers' Rights Act 2019.
rights.protectionDesc: Your employer cannot terminate your employment during pregnancy or maternity leave.
rights.breastfeedingDesc: You are entitled to two 30-minute breaks per day for breastfeeding until your child is 6 months old.
rights.medicalDesc: You have the right to time off for prenatal medical appointments.
rights.source: Source: Workers' Rights Act 2019, Government of Mauritius
rights.leaveDetail1: 14 weeks of maternity leave paid at 100% of your base salary.
rights.leaveDetail2: You can start your leave up to 6 weeks before your expected delivery date.
rights.leaveDetail3: After delivery, you are entitled to a minimum of 8 weeks of leave.
rights.leaveDetail4: If you have complications, your doctor can prescribe additional sick leave that will not be deducted from your 14 weeks.
rights.leaveDetail5: This right applies to all workers, whether permanent, contract, or part-time.
rights.protectionDetail1: Your employer CANNOT dismiss you during your pregnancy or during your maternity leave.
rights.protectionDetail2: If you are dismissed during this period, it is an abusive dismissal subject to legal action.
rights.protectionDetail3: Your employer cannot deny you a promotion because of your pregnancy.
rights.protectionDetail4: Your working conditions cannot be changed negatively (reduced salary, changed schedule without your agreement).
rights.protectionDetail5: If this happens to you, contact the Ministry of Labour immediately: 207 2600.
rights.breastfeedingDetail1: 2 breaks of 30 minutes per day for breastfeeding.
rights.breastfeedingDetail2: These breaks are paid — they cannot be deducted from your salary.
rights.breastfeedingDetail3: This right is valid until your baby is 6 months old.
rights.breastfeedingDetail4: Your employer must provide a private and clean space for breastfeeding or expressing milk.
rights.breastfeedingDetail5: These breaks are in addition to your normal lunch break.
rights.medicalDetail1: You have the right to attend your prenatal appointments without loss of salary.
rights.medicalDetail2: You must present a medical certificate to your employer.
rights.medicalDetail3: This includes ultrasounds, blood tests, and all prenatal consultations.
rights.medicalDetail4: Your employer cannot refuse you these appointments.
rights.medicalDetail5: Tip: inform your employer as far in advance as possible for appointments.
rights.emergencyContact: If your rights are not respected, contact the Ministry of Labour
rights.emergencyContactDesc: Confidential and free. They can help you assert your rights.
rights.faqSubtitle: Answers to common questions about maternity rights in Mauritius
rights.faqQuestion1: My employer refuses to give me maternity leave. What do I do?
rights.faqAnswer1: Contact the Ministry of Labour immediately on 207 2600. This is illegal and your employer risks sanctions. You can also contact a lawyer specialising in labour law. Keep all evidence (emails, messages, etc.).
rights.faqQuestion2: I have a fixed-term contract (CDD). Am I entitled to maternity leave?
rights.faqAnswer2: Yes! All workers, whether permanent, contract, or part-time, are entitled to maternity leave if they have worked at least 12 consecutive months with the same employer.
rights.faqQuestion3: Can I work overtime during my pregnancy?
rights.faqAnswer3: You are not obliged to work overtime during your pregnancy. If your work requires you to do things that are dangerous for your health or your baby's health, your doctor can prescribe a certificate to change your working conditions.
rights.faqQuestion4: What happens if my baby is born premature?
rights.faqAnswer4: Your maternity leave starts from the day of delivery. Your 14 weeks remain the same. If your baby needs to be hospitalised, you can discuss an arrangement with your employer.
rights.faqQuestion5: I'm a father. Is there paternity leave?
rights.faqAnswer5: Yes! Since 2019, fathers are entitled to 5 days of paid paternity leave. This must be taken within 5 weeks after the birth of the baby.
rights.faqQuestion6: I had a miscarriage. Am I entitled to leave?
rights.faqAnswer6: Yes, you are entitled to 14 days of paid leave if you have a miscarriage after week 14 of pregnancy. If before week 14, your doctor can prescribe sick leave.
rights.ctaDesc: Contact the Ministry of Labour for free and confidential advice on your specific situation.
